versionNotes: Sprint 4 W13 Three-Sprint Retrospective v2 — 3 core questions with AI sparring + 8 optional dimension questions. Replaces the v1 8-identical-prompt structure with an interview-grounded reflection.
title: Three-Sprint Retrospective
description: ~20 minutes. You leave with a map of where you actually grew vs where learning didn't land — plus a draft answer to 'how do you learn?' that you could use in a job interview. This is the input to your Gap Statement.

questions.infoBlock:
Core 1 of 3 · Real growth, real evidence

Identify where you grew this semester, with evidence.

Evidence means something you can point to: a specific assignment, a moment in class, a piece of feedback, or a comparison between Week 2 you and Week 12 you. “I got better at reflection” isn’t evidence — “my Week 7 reflection made me sit with a failure pattern I’d been moving past for years” is.
questions.prompt: Identify where you grew this semester, with evidence.
questions.placeholder: Name the growth and the specific evidence that shows it...
questions.sparringRole: Is the evidence concrete enough, or still in generalities? Push the student to name a specific assignment, moment, or before/after comparison. If they've named a real artifact, affirm it and ask what made it the turning point.

questions.sectionTitle: Where learning didn't connect, and why
questions.infoBlock:
Core 2 of 3 · What didn’t land

Identify where learning didn’t connect for you this semester, and why.

Not every part of this semester will have landed — that’s information, not failure. Maybe an assignment felt abstract, maybe you were stretched thin, maybe the skill wasn’t ready yet, maybe you checked out and knew it. All of those are valid answers. The “why” matters more than the “what.”
questions.prompt: Identify where learning didn't connect for you this semester, and why.
questions.placeholder: Name what didn't connect and be honest about why...
questions.sparringRole: The student has given a clean reason — is that the full story, or the nicer version? Gently push for the messier underlying cause without being accusatory. If they've already named something hard, affirm the honesty and ask what they'd do differently next time.

questions.sectionTitle: How you learn — the interview version
questions.infoBlock:
Core 3 of 3 · The interview answer

What would you actually answer to a behavioral interview question about how you learn?

Interviewers ask things like “Tell me about a time you had to learn something unfamiliar” or “How do you handle problems you’ve never seen before?” Your answer reveals more about you than your GPA. What would you actually say — using this semester as your evidence? Write it the way you’d say it out loud.
questions.prompt: What would you actually answer to a behavioral interview question about how you learn?
questions.placeholder: Write this as you'd say it in an interview. Use Q1 and Q2 as evidence...
questions.sparringRole: Play a mock interviewer. Ask natural follow-ups like 'Can you give me that first sentence you'd actually say?' or 'What would you cut if you only had 60 seconds?' or 'Where does your voice sound rehearsed vs real?' — help the student tighten the answer into something they'd confidently say out loud.

questions.infoBlock:
Optional depth

These 8 dimensions are optional depth. Answer any that help you think, or skip them all.

The 3 required questions above are the core. Each dimension below asks one honest question — 60 characters minimum if you choose to answer. Use them as a thinking aid for your Gap Statement, or skip straight to export.
questions.prompt: The 8 questions below are optional. Answer any that sharpen your thinking. Skip the rest.

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 1 of 8 · Goals

Were you working toward something specific this semester, or mostly just meeting deadlines?
questions.prompt: Were you working toward something specific this semester, or mostly just meeting deadlines? Name one moment that proves it.

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 2 of 8 · Planning

How did you plan your time this semester?
questions.prompt: How did you plan your time this semester? When did planning work for you, and when did it fall apart?
questions.placeholder: When it worked and when it didn't...

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 3 of 8 · Ambiguity

A moment you didn’t know what to do — what did you do with that feeling?
questions.prompt: Name one moment you didn't know what to do. What did you do with that feeling?

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 4 of 8 · Metacognition

Noticing yourself working badly or avoiding something.
questions.prompt: When did you notice yourself working badly or avoiding something? Did you do anything with that noticing?

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 5 of 8 · Iteration

Real revision — not just re-reading.
questions.prompt: Name one piece of work you actually revised (not just re-read). What changed and why?

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 6 of 8 · Evaluation

How you knew your work was good — or not.
questions.prompt: How did you know your work was good — or not? Did you trust the rubric, the instructor, or yourself?

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 7 of 8 · Collaboration

Peers making your work better — and worse.
questions.prompt: When did peers make your work better this semester? When did working with others make it worse?

questions.infoBlock:
Dimension 8 of 8 · AI use

Specific moments only — no generalities.
questions.prompt: When did AI make your work stronger? When did it make it weaker? Specific moments only.
